1: A precise date formula — the fourth year of Jehoiakim = 605 BCE, the year of the Battle of Carchemish, when Nebuchadnezzar decisively defeated Egypt and became the dominant power in the region. Jeremiah places his ministry in the sweep of international history.
2: Jeremiah speaks to all the people of Judah and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem — the universal audience mirrors the universal scope of the coming judgment; no one is exempt from hearing or from the covenant's consequences.
3: מִשְּׁלֹשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה (mishlosh esreh shanah, from the thirteenth year): Jeremiah's call was in the 13th year of Josiah (1:2) — making this 23 years of continuous prophesying. The length of service emphasizes the depth of Israel's stubborn refusal.
4: הַשְׁכֵּם וְשָׁלֹחַ (hashkem veshaloach, rising early and sending): the infinitive absolute construction used repeatedly in Jeremiah for YHWH's persistent sending of his prophets — rising early connotes eager, urgent, repeated action. Despite this diligence, the people have not listened (lo sham'u) or inclined their ear (lo hitu oznekhem).
5: The core demand of the prophets: turn (shuvu) each from his evil way and evil deeds (ma'alelav hara'im), and dwell in the land that YHWH gave. The covenant offer was perpetually available — prophets were not bringing new information but calling back to what was already known.
6: Do not go after other gods to serve and bow down to them — the Shema's exclusive loyalty demand restated. Do not provoke YHWH with the work of your hands (bema'aseh yedekhem) — the idol-making described in ch 10 as provoking YHWH directly.
7: You have not listened to me — YHWH's verdict after 23 years. The idol-worship provokes YHWH (le-ma'an hach'is oti) to their own harm (lera' lakhem). The self-destructive nature of apostasy: it harms the people, not primarily YHWH.
8: Therefore — the covenant consequence clause (laken) follows the indictment. Because you have not heard my words, the judgment follows necessarily from the structure established in the covenant curses of Deuteronomy.
9: עַבְדִּי (avdi, my servant): Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon is called YHWH's servant — one of the most striking titles in Jeremiah. The same title is given to the prophets ('my servants the prophets', v4) and to David (Ps 89:3). YHWH claims sovereignty over the greatest earthly power by incorporating it into his covenantal purposes. חֶרֶב (cherev, sword), רָעָב (ra'av, famine), דֶּבֶר (dever, pestilence) — the classic covenant-curse triad of Lev 26:25-26 and Deut 28:21-22.
10: YHWH will banish the sound of joy (qol sason), gladness (qol simcha), the voice of the bride (qol chatan vekol kallah), and the sound of the millstone (qol rechayim) — the sounds of normal life. The absence of wedding sounds and grinding sounds signals complete social breakdown. Cf. Rev 18:22-23 where these same sounds mark the fall of Babylon.
11: שִׁבְעִים שָׁנָה (shivim shanah, seventy years): the exile duration. The number may be symbolic (a human lifetime, a sabbath of decades) or roughly chronological (605-538 BCE = 67 years). Daniel 9:2 understood it literally and prayed toward its fulfillment. The point is the fixed, purposeful nature of the judgment — not open-ended but bounded by YHWH's plan.
12: After seventy years, YHWH will punish (epoqod, visit in judgment) the king of Babylon and that nation — making it an eternal wasteland (shemamot olamim). The same judgment standard applied to Judah is applied to Babylon: serving as YHWH's instrument does not exempt Babylon from accountability for its own cruelty.
13: YHWH will bring on that land everything written in this book — the self-referential note signals that the Book of Jeremiah is itself a covenantal document, its words carrying the weight of treaty stipulations that will be enforced.
14: Many nations and great kings will make servants of Babylon — YHWH will repay (shillamti) Babylon according to its deeds. The lex talionis principle: the enslaver will be enslaved; the nation that made others serve will itself serve. Cf. Jer 50:29 and Rev 18:6.
15: כּוֹס הַיַּיִן הַחֵמָה (kos hayyayin hachemmah, the cup of the wine of wrath): kos (cup) is a powerful covenant metaphor — the cup can be a cup of salvation (Ps 116:13) or a cup of wrath. Chemmah (wrath/heat/poison) makes this the latter: YHWH's measured judgment poured out as a drink. The cup-of-wrath motif becomes central to NT Christology: Jesus drinks this cup at Gethsemane (Luke 22:42); Revelation 14:10 and 16:19 apply it to eschatological judgment.
16: The nations who drink the cup will stagger (vega'ashu) and be driven out of their minds (vehit-holelu) because of the sword YHWH sends among them. The drunkenness-of-judgment image links to the wineskin parable of ch 13 — the nations are filled with YHWH's wrath as wineskins filled with wine.
17: Jeremiah takes the cup from YHWH's hand and makes all the nations drink — the prophet's role as the divine instrument who delivers the cup of judgment. The listing of nations follows (vv18-26), culminating with Babylon itself (v26), showing that no empire is exempt from the cup.
18: Jerusalem and the cities of Judah are the first to drink — YHWH's judgment begins with his own household (cf. 1 Pet 4:17: 'judgment begins with the household of God'). This is not a contradiction of YHWH's love for Israel but a confirmation of covenant accountability — those who know the most bear the greatest responsibility.
19: Pharaoh king of Egypt and his servants — Egypt, the power from which Israel was delivered, is also subject to the cup. No previous relationship with YHWH exempts a nation from judgment; Egypt's past role as exodus-context does not shield it.
20: The land of Uz and the kings of Philistia — Uz is Job's homeland (Job 1:1), suggesting even that region of ancient wisdom falls under the cup. The Philistine cities (Ashkelon, Gaza, Ekron, Ashdod) are specific; this is the historical geography of Canaan's covenant territory.
21: Edom, Moab, Ammon — Israel's neighboring kinship nations (descendants of Esau and Lot) are included; kinship to Israel provides no exemption from the judgment. All the Transjordanian nations drink the cup.
22: The kings of Tyre, Sidon, and the coastland islands — the Phoenician maritime powers who controlled Mediterranean trade. Their wealth and seafaring power is no protection against the cup of YHWH's wrath.
23: Dedan, Tema, Buz, and all who cut the corners of their hair (qotzei pe'ah) — the Arab tribes who practiced this specific hair-cutting rite (also mentioned in 9:26 and 49:32); Jeremiah includes even the remote desert peoples in the universal scope of judgment.
24: All the kings of Arabia and the mixed peoples (ha'erev) who live in the desert — the totality of the Arabian Peninsula's populations. The list keeps extending to encompass all known peoples.
25: Zimri, Elam, and the Medes — eastern nations beyond Mesopotamia. Elam will receive its own oracle in ch 49; the Medes will be the instruments of Babylon's fall (50:41-43; Isa 13:17-19). Their current inclusion in the cup anticipates their later role.
26: שֵׁשַׁךְ (Sheshakh) — a cryptogram (atbash: sh-sh-k = b-b-l in reversed Hebrew alphabet) for Babylon (Bavel). Babylon drinks the cup last — the nation that administers judgment is itself not exempt. The cup returns to its source. Cf. Rev 18:6.
27: שְׁתוּ וְשִׁכְרוּ וְקִיאוּ (shetu veshikru vekiy'u, drink, get drunk, vomit): the physical degradation of the nations under divine judgment — not a dignified defeat but a shameful collapse. The sword (cherev) that YHWH is sending among them follows.
28: If they refuse to take the cup from your hand to drink — they will still drink: שָׁתֹה תִשְׁתּוּ (shato tishthu): the emphatic infinitive absolute — you will certainly drink. Refusal is not an option; the covenant judgment is not negotiable.
29: הָעִיר אֲשֶׁר נִקְרָא שְׁמִי עָלֶיהָ (ha'ir asher niqra shemi aleha, the city that bears my name): Jerusalem — the city of YHWH's name. YHWH begins with his own name-city; if Jerusalem is not exempt, how can any other city expect exemption? The rhetorical force: the cup is universal because YHWH is the universal judge.
30: יִשְׁאַג (yish'ag, he will roar): YHWH roaring from his holy dwelling (me'on qodsho) like a lion over his pasture — the lion is no longer the animal Israel feared from the Jordan thickets (12:8) but YHWH himself. הֵידָד כְּדֹרְכִים יַעֲנֶה (heydad kedorkheim ya'aneh, shouting like those who tread the grapes): YHWH's voice over the earth like the harvest shout — judgment is the harvest of what Israel and the nations have sown.
31: בָּא שָׁאוֹן עַד-קְצֵה הָאָרֶץ (ba sha'on ad-qetzeh ha'aretz, a din reaches the ends of the earth): the cosmic scope of judgment — the whole earth is in the range of YHWH's court case (riv). YHWH has an indictment against all flesh; the wicked will be given to the sword.
32: Disaster is spreading from nation to nation — מִיַּרְכְּתֵי הָאָרֶץ (miyarketey ha'aretz, from the ends/farthest parts of the earth): the judgment is not localized but global in its sweep. The great storm (sa'ar gadol) is rising from the ends of the earth.
33: The slain of YHWH on that day will cover the face of the whole earth — מִקְצֵה הָאָרֶץ וְעַד-קְצֵהוּ (miqtzeh ha'aretz ve'ad-qetzehu, from one end of the earth to the other). They will not be mourned, gathered, or buried — dung (domen) on the face of the ground. The dishonor of unburied death is a covenant curse (Deut 28:26).
34: הֵילִילוּ הָרֹעִים (heililu haro'im, wail, you shepherds): the ro'im (shepherds/rulers) are commanded to wail because their days of slaughter and scattering are coming. The leaders who failed their flocks will be first to mourn.
35: וְאָבַד מָנוֹס מִן-הָרֹעִים (ve'avad manos min-haro'im, flight will perish from the shepherds): no escape for the leaders who scattered the flock. The usual options of flight and survival are cut off — covenant judgment is inescapable for those who bore the highest responsibility.
36: קוֹל צַעֲקַת הָרֹעִים וִילְלַת אַדִּירֵי הַצֹּאן (qol tza'aqat haro'im viylalat addire hatzzon, sound of the cry of the shepherds and the wailing of the leaders of the flock): the lamentation of the ruling class. YHWH is ruining (shochet) their pasture because of the fierce anger of the oppressor.
37: The peaceful meadows (nevot hashalom) are devastated because of YHWH's fierce anger. The covenant blessing of peaceful pastures (Ps 23; Ezek 34:14-15) is reversed: the anger that should bring rain and abundance brings desolation.
38: עָזַב כַּכְּפִיר סֻכֹּה (azav kakfir sukkoh, he has left his den like a young lion): YHWH has left his divine dwelling to come in judgment — a terrifying reversal. When YHWH dwells in Zion, his presence is protection; when he departs and comes against the nations, it is destruction. The land is made a desolation because of the oppressor's sword and YHWH's fierce anger (charon apo).
